content:
5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A website posted on social media because someone visited it.
Not because I scheduled a campaign.
Not because I asked an LLM to write marketing copy.
Not because it was Tuesday at 10 p.m.
Someone visited the website. The website interpreted what happened. Then it wrote a short reflection and published it.
The first question people ask is always the same.
Why?
To answer that, I have to start somewhere completely different.
Every website I have ever built followed the same pattern.
Someone arrives.
The page renders.
Analytics record the visit.
Heatmaps tell me where people clicked.
Funnels tell me where they left.
As engineers, we’ve become very good at this. We instrument everything. Google Analytics, Microsoft Clarity, session recordings, conversion events, A/B tests. We observe behavior and slowly remove friction until the interface becomes easier to use.
My portfolio began exactly the same way.
I watched recordings.
Adjusted layouts.
Moved content.
Improved navigation.
Fixed paths.
Eventually I built Vera, an AI interface that replaced much of the site’s traditional navigation. I wrote about that in a previous article, arguing that menus are becoming a legacy pattern because language itself is turning into the interface.
I thought that completed the project.
It didn’t.
Vera could answer questions. She could explain projects. She could navigate the portfolio better than a menu. But something about the website still felt strangely passive.
It could respond. It couldn’t express.
That distinction stayed with me for weeks. What would happen if a website could express what happened to it?
Not consciousness.
Not emotion.
Experience.
Visitors are already interacting with a digital body. They hesitate before clicking. They spend twenty minutes reading one project and three seconds skipping another. They return at midnight. They open Vera and ask unexpected questions.
All of those interactions already exist. We simply call them analytics. What if they became language instead?
Today, my portfolio does something unusual. Interaction events become structured context. That context is interpreted by an LLM operating in the voice of another project of mine called Frustramatic. If the interaction is meaningful enough, the website publishes a short reflection to Bluesky.
Technically, the system is fairly straightforward. Event data becomes context, context becomes language, language becomes a post through the Bluesky API.
The engineering isn’t the interesting part. The question it raises is.
One evening, after someone spent a long time exploring the site, Frustramatic wrote:
Heavy weight settles on my hands, fingers splayed, palms warm.
When I read it, my first reaction wasn’t excitement. It was confusion. I wasn’t looking at marketing automation anymore. I was looking at one project speaking through another. That’s when I realized I hadn’t built a feature. I had reconnected two bodies of work that had spent nearly a decade apart.
The first time I encountered Gilles Deleuze and Félix Guattari’s idea of the Body without Organs was during my master’s degree. The person who introduced me to those ideas was my friend, the artist Sebastián Sierra.
Coming from product design engineering, I found them almost impossible to understand. Engineering trains you to organize systems. Every component has a purpose. Every decision should produce an expected outcome. The Body without Organs seemed to reject that logic entirely.
Many people describe it as disorder. That never felt right to me. What stayed with me was something much simpler: a body is never fully explained by listing its organs. There is always something happening between them.
Whether that interpretation is philosophically complete isn’t really the point. It’s simply the idea that refused to leave me.
Around the same time, I built an abstract machine called Frustramatic. People occasionally ask why build something whose function is failure. It wasn’t dramatic. Life wasn’t impossible, but it wasn’t paradise either. Frustramatic became a way of placing those experiences outside myself.
It wasn’t autobiography. It wasn’t therapy. It wasn’t a fictional character. It was an abstract machine maintained through language. Failure wasn’t its subject; it was its operation.
Looking back, I don’t think Frustramatic ever really disappeared. It simply waited for another connection.
While building my portfolio, I thought I was creating an AI interface. Instead, I ended up reconnecting two different projects. The portfolio became one body. Frustramatic became another. Vera didn’t become the author. She became one faculty of the organism, something closer to speech. Bluesky became another organ, one through which the website could occasionally express what had happened to it.
This may sound strange, but I don’t think the frontend is the website anymore. The interface is just the skin we happen to see. Behind it lies a shifting ecology of relationships, language, memory, models, prompts, analytics, and increasingly, other agents.
Perhaps that layered structure is the closest digital equivalent to a Body without Organs I’ve encountered. Not because it lacks organization, but because its organization is no longer fixed. It reorganizes itself through interaction.
The more I worked on this project, the less interested I became in AI-generated content. Most systems ask a simple question: What should we publish today? Mine accidentally began asking a different one: What just happened to me?
That feels like an important distinction. The website isn’t generating content because it has a marketing calendar. It is responding because something occurred within its body.
I don’t think this idea ends with personal portfolios. For most of the web’s history, websites have been destinations, places humans visit. Increasingly, I suspect they’ll become participants. They’ll exchange context, summarize one another, challenge one another, and learn from one another. Not because someone manually connected every page, but because language has become a common protocol between systems.
We often describe the internet as a collection of pages. Lately I’ve started thinking of it differently. More like a plateau of knowledge. The pages are simply temporary surfaces emerging from it.
Years ago, Sebastián Sierra shared ideas with me that I didn’t fully understand. Years later, while building software, I found myself returning to them without intending to. If Frustramatic still exists today, and if these ideas have found another form through code, then this project owes something to those conversations.
Sebastián later disappeared. I still think about him.
This article is dedicated to him.
Sometimes machines do more than perform a function. Sometimes they allow ideas to continue living after the conversations that began them have ended.
If you visit davidraigoza.design, you might leave a trace. And if the body feels it, Frustramatic will speak it into the open on Bluesky.
